context_update.summary: The front matter and introduction section establish the motivation for using machine learning to predict microstructure in low-density FeMnAlC austenitic steels. Key material system: Fe-Mn-Al-C with typical composition ranges of 18-30 wt% Mn, 8-12 wt% Al, 0.6-1.8 wt% C. The main phases include γ austenite, κ-carbide, α-ferrite, B2/DO3 intermetallics, and β-Mn. Important processing variable: aging temperature 500-600 °C. The study’s ML targets are four characteristics: κ existence, (γ+κ) region at aging temperature, single γ region, and κ volume fraction. Al and C are identified as dominant influencing elements, with specific thresholds predicted (e.g., at 30 wt% Mn, a minimum of about 4 wt% Al is needed for κ). The experimental validation alloy is Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C. This chunk provides foundational knowledge on composition–microstructure relationships, mechanisms of alloying elements, and the ML design framework (Fig. 1). Subsequent chunks will likely detail dataset construction, model building, and experimental validation results.
mechanisms: Al decreases C diffusivity, improves SFE, stabilizes α, inhibits γ→ε, and reduces density
Mn stabilizes γ and excess Mn forms β-Mn
C stabilizes γ and promotes κ precipitation
κ-carbides are coherent ordered precipitates aging at 500-600 °C and are sheared by dislocations on {111}<110>

context_update.summary: This chunk describes the Methods section of the study, establishing the machine learning design framework for low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels. The models target four features: κ phase existence (Y1, classification), (γ+κ) dual-phase region at 500–600 °C (Y2, classification), single γ phase region (Y3, classification), and κ phase volume fraction (Y4, regression). The dataset contains 448 samples collected from literature and JMatPro v9.0 phase-balance calculations, covering a composition space of 10–30 wt% Mn, 2–12 wt% Al, and 0.2–2 wt% C. Each sample has 10 features: the elemental contents of Fe, Mn, Al, C, plus six descriptors (VEC, δ, ΔHmix, ΔSmix, Tm, Δχ) defined in Table 1. Binary labels (1/0) are used for Y1–Y3; missing data elimination reduces Y4 to 298 samples. Machine learning algorithms mentioned include KNN, ANN, CART, SVM, and RF. Ten-fold cross-validation is applied to prevent overfitting, and regression models are evaluated with R, R², MAE, and RMSE. This chunk provides the essential methodology, dataset characteristics, and modeling targets that will be used in subsequent model training and validation sections.

context_update.summary: This chunk first completes the methodology section by specifying k-fold cross-validation for classification and regression, then details the experimental procedure for validating machine learning predictions using a Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C (wt.%) steel. The processing route includes homogenization at 1200°C/2h, forging to 60×60mm, re-homogenization at 1050°C/100min, hot rolling to 12mm, air cooling, 75% cold reduction, annealing at 1000°C/15min, water quenching, and aging at 550°C/2h. Characterization methods are given: XRD (30–100° 2θ, 3°/min), SEM, EPMA, TEM at 200kV, EBSD sample prep, and uniaxial tensile tests (1×10⁻³ s⁻¹ strain rate, gauge 10×4×2mm). The Results section begins with descriptor selection: Δχ and δ are removed due to PCC>0.95 (Fig. 2). For classification, RF is selected as the best algorithm with 10-fold CVA of 0.98 (Y1,Y2) and 0.96 (Y3) (Fig. 3a). Relative importance analysis shows Al is most critical for single γ and κ existence, while Mn and Fe dominate the (γ+κ) region at 500–600°C (Fig. 3b–d). Mechanistic insights link Al to BCC stabilization and κ precipitation, and Mn to γ stabilization and β-Mn formation. For regression, RF shows RMSE 1.8 (Fig. 4a). A screening flowchart reduces 78,477 candidates to 150 by requiring κ existence, single γ, (γ+κ) region, κ fraction ≥10%, and density ≤7 g/cm³ (Fig. 5). Subsequent chunks are expected to present the predicted composition regions and experimental validation microstructures.
microstructure: κ phase precipitation initiated at Al>~5 wt% when Mn=30 wt%
mechanisms: Al reduces C diffusivity and stabilizes κ phase
Al is BCC-stabilizing and increases FCC lattice parameter, destabilizing single-phase austenite
Mn stabilizes γ phase and increases FCC lattice parameter
Mn content between 5-12% favors γ→ε→αʹ, and 15-30% favors γ→ε
excess Mn may promote β-Mn phase

context_update.summary: The chunk details descriptor importance for classification models: Al is the most critical for single γ and κ existence, while Mn and Fe dominate (γ+κ) region classification at 500–600°C; C has minimal influence on κ presence. Regression using SVM achieves cross-validation RMSE <1% with descriptor order ΔSmix, Fe, ΔHmix, C, Mn, T, VEC, Al. A two-stage screening of 78,477 candidates yields 1320 satisfying Y1/Y2/Y3, then 150 with κ >10% and density ≤7 g/cm³; further tightening to κ≥11% and density ≤6.9 g/cm³ gives 30 candidates. Composition-structure maps at 30% Mn define κ existence (Al>4–4.5%), (γ+κ) region (Al 4.5–9.3%, C 0.4–1.1%), and a single γ boundary highly sensitive at C=1.1%. Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C aged at 550°C is predicted to be single-phase austenite with low density and high strength and was selected for validation. Fig. 8 presents XRD, EBSD, and TEM images of this alloy. Upcoming chunks are expected to provide detailed microstructural and mechanical property results for the validation alloy.
key_variables: descriptors: ΔSmix, Fe, ΔHmix, C, Mn, T, VEC, Al
properties: predicted low density and high strength for Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C

Al critical value 4–4.5% triggers κ precipitation at 30% Mn
C is austenite stabilizer; 1.1% C is critical for single γ boundary
Mn stabilizes γ and can promote β-Mn
γ→ε favored at 15–30% Mn, γ→ε→αʹ at 5–12% Mn

context_update.summary: This chunk presents the experimental validation of the ML-designed Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C low-density steel. The measured composition is Fe-30.4Mn-7.9Al-1.1C (wt%). The alloy aged at 550 °C exhibits a recrystallized single FCC phase microstructure with an average grain size of 17 μm (EBSD, Fig. 8b-c) and only FCC XRD peaks (Fig. 8a). TEM confirms nanoscale κ‑carbide precipitates formed during aging (Fig. 8d-e), consistent with the predicted (γ+κ) dual-phase region at 500–600 °C; water cooling did not suppress κ formation. The fine κ-carbides contribute to significant precipitation strengthening. Mechanical testing reveals yield strength 953 MPa, tensile strength 1109 MPa, and elongation 42% (Fig. 9b). EPMA shows uniform element distribution, supporting the single-phase nature (Fig. 9a). The conclusions reiterate that Al and C are key for phase control: at 30 wt% Mn, κ phase is retained with ≥4 wt% Al, the (γ+κ) region spans Al 4.5–9.3 wt% and C 0.4–1.1 wt% between 500–600 °C, and a single γ region requires ≤11.6 wt% Al. These results validate the design approach. Subsequent chunks may cover in-depth mechanical behavior, deformation mechanisms, or further processing-property links.
processing_route: hot rolling, cold rolling (as previously described) leading to recrystallized microstructure
mechanisms: precipitation strengthening by nano-sized intragranular κ-carbide
Al stabilizes κ phase and regulates phase component
C and Al contents determine coexistence of γ and κ

The fine κ-carbides contribute to significant precipitation strengthening. Mechanical testing reveals yield strength 953 MPa, tensile strength 1109 MPa, and elongation 42% (Fig. 9b). EPMA shows uniform element distribution, supporting the single-phase nature (Fig. 9a). The conclusions reiterate that Al and C are key for phase control: at 30 wt% Mn, κ phase is retained with ≥4 wt% Al, the (γ+κ) region spans Al 4.5–9.3 wt% and C 0.4–1.1 wt% between 500–600 °C, and a single γ region requires ≤11.6 wt% Al. These results validate the design approach. Subsequent chunks may cover in-depth mechanical behavior, deformation mechanisms, or further processing-property links.
Material systems: Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel system; 18-30 wt% Mn, 8-12 wt% Al, 0.6-1.8 wt% C; Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel; (100-x-y-z)Fe-xMn-yAl-zC system; Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C (wt.%) experimental alloy; Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C (validation alloy); Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C (nominal); Fe-30.4Mn-7.9Al-1.1C (measured)
Sample aliases: Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C steel; Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C; validation component; Fe-30.4Mn-7.9Al-1.1C
Processing route: aging at 500-600 °C; induction melting under Ar; homogenization 1200°C 2h; forging to 60mm × 60mm billet; homogenization 1050°C 100min; hot rolling to 12mm thick plates; air cooling; cold rolling with 75% reduction
Key variables: Mn content (wt%); Al content (wt%); C content (wt%); aging temperature; Mn content (x): 10-30 wt%; Al content (y): 2-12 wt%; C content (z): 0.2-2 wt%; Y1: κ existence classification
Microstructure: γ austenite; κ-carbide; α-ferrite; B2/DO3 intermetallics; β-Mn phase; κ phase; γ+κ dual-phase; single γ phase
Properties: SVM regression RMSE < 1%; κ fraction >10% and ≥11% in screening; density ≤7 g/cm³ and ≤6.9 g/cm³; predicted low density and high strength for Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C; yield strength 953 MPa; tensile strength 1109 MPa; elongation 42%; low density (implied)
Mechanisms: Al decreases C diffusivity, improves SFE, stabilizes α, inhibits γ→ε, and reduces density; Mn stabilizes γ and excess Mn forms β-Mn; C stabilizes γ and promotes κ precipitation; κ-carbides are coherent ordered precipitates aging at 500-600 °C and are sheared by dislocations on {111}<110>; Al reduces C diffusivity and stabilizes κ phase; Al is BCC-stabilizing and increases FCC lattice parameter, destabilizing single-phase austenite; Mn stabilizes γ phase and increases FCC lattice parameter; Mn content between 5-12% favors γ→ε→αʹ, and 15-30% favors γ→ε
